---
title: "S1: Finite Circle Core"
---

The S1 core uses finite `C_n` objects as cyclic address spaces. In v0, these are combinatorial circles, not Euclidean metric circles.

This unit is the first place where Circle Calculus becomes usable. It teaches a small loop of addresses, then builds motion, repetition, closure, and period on top of that loop.

::: {.learning-unit}

## Unit Goal

By the end of S1, a reader should be able to look at a finite circle `C_n`, choose a stride `k`, predict the orbit, compute the period from `gcd(n,k)`, and explain which parts are intuition, executable examples, or Lean-proved theorems.

:::

## Lesson Order

1. finite circles and nodes,
2. rotations as modular addition,
3. coils, orbits, closure, and period,
4. gcd decomposition and prime full coils,
5. winding lift as a separate residue-plus-turns layer.

## Mental Picture

Imagine `n` labeled positions arranged around a loop. The geometry of the drawn loop is only a picture; the actual object is the address rule that sends every integer to one of those `n` labels. Rotation means adding a stride and reducing again. A coil is what you see when the same stride is repeated until the motion closes.

The first four lessons use interactive diagrams so you can change `n`, starts, and strides. The diagrams are there to make the theorem statements feel concrete before you open the proof trail.

::: {.checkpoint-box}

## First Checkpoint

Before moving to higher dimensions, make sure you can answer this without looking at source code: in `C_12`, why does stride `4` visit `0, 4, 8` and then close, while stride `5` visits all twelve addresses?

:::

<div class="dimension-banner" data-dimension-id="S1">S1 finite cyclic address core</div>

<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="S1-0001"></div>

<script type="module" src="../../widgets/shared/widget_base.js"></script>
